filename: How to manage social eating events PTC.pdf
source: How to manage social eating events PTC.pdf
strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text:
1

HOW TO MANAGE SOCIAL
EATING EVENTS

2

The average person gains a pound of fat during Christmas and similar social eating
events. Here are 13 tips to enjoy these kinds of 'cheat meals' without getting fat.

Note: The scientific references for all tips are provided in our online PT Course. Ask
your coach if you're curious about a specific topic.

1. Do your research
Why do your meals fit your macros? Because you planned the meals. Restaurant meals
are no different. If you go in unprepared, there's a good chance you're going to screw
your diet. Not only that, but you can't know if they have anything you want in the first
place if you haven't checked their menu. So always check a place's menu before you go
there. You can often find it on their website, Facebook page, TripAdvisor or Zomato.

While you're full, undistracted and not under social pressure, decide what you want to
eat. This allows for much more rational choices than when you make them in the hunger
of the moment.

Many restaurants don't have good low-calorie filler options or no pre-set meals that
perfectly fit your wishes. In this case you should call them and inform them of your
special needs. Before you're there, you have a much stronger negotiation position, so
don't be afraid to make demands. You're paying them to make food exactly as you like
it, which brings us to the next tip.

3

2. Don't be a slave to the menu
If you're eating out in a restaurant, most people just order what's on the menu because
they're too afraid to order anything else. Don't be a sheep. You can often save a ton of
calories with some simple modifications to a meal. Here are some example tips.
• Ask the chef to use as little oil as possible. This can save several hundred
calories, especially for grilled food. Many chefs use absurd amounts of oils and
butters in virtually any recipe. It makes the food easier to prepare, but the extra
calories are often not worth it. Research finds people are often unable to tell the
difference between oil-loaded and oil-light meals when blinded.
• Ask if they have low calorie sweeteners that they can use instead of sugar. This
can again save several hundred more calories. Even better, you can also ask to
skip the sugar and bring your own sweetener. Menno brings sucralose powder or
liquid to every social eating event.
• Ask to replace side-dishes like French fries or bread with baked/boiled potatoes
or vegetables. You can also ask for extra large side servings of these foods as
fillers, as most restaurants serve do-you-even-lift portion sizes.

If they don't have anything you deem worthy of the calories or the food quality, you can
almost always just order a nice piece of steak, fish or poultry with steamed or grilled
vegetables. No reason to consume a lot of calories if you don't want to.

Too afraid of what people will think? Man up. You have to earn a good physique.
headings: 2. Don’t be a slave to the menu

pages.text:
4

3. Save up calories, but don't starve yourself
Energy balance determines whether your body will store energy. Eating junkfood by
itself won't magically make you gain weight. It's the calories that are most important. So
if you keep most of your day's meals very low in calories - think lean protein sources,
vegetables and fibrous fruits - you can save up a lot of calories for a big dinner.

As a reference, most full restaurant meals contain around 1300 calories with normal
portion sizes, regardless of country. Not too many 'normal' meals go over 2000 kcal and
almost no meals contain fewer than 600 kcal.

Importantly, you don't want to starve yourself before the meal. Eating nothing but
protein shakes to save up calories is a classic mistake, as you'll be starving before the
cheat meal and then do heinous damage. As a rough framework, you can think of your
appetite as having 100 points each day. You need to hit those 100 satiety points or
you'll be hungry. Foods differ in terms of how satiating they are, but they differ much
more in terms of how caloric they are. If you only filled up 10 points before a cheat meal
and you fill up the remaining 90 points with high calorie foods, you'll probably massively
overeat. Conversely, if you ate soups, egg white omelets and other satiating but low-
calorie foods beforehand, you may only need to fill in 30 satiety points during the cheat
meal and you can't do that much damage. In other words, the best state to be in before
a cheat meal is stuffed!

Tip: You can consume a big serving of our 3-minute tomato soup before a meal for
fewer than 100 calories.

Plus, when you're already full, you're in a perfect state of mind to make rational
decisions about which foods are worth it. Remember, the point of the cheat meal should
be to eat to satiety and enjoy the food, not to set a Guiness World Record for amount of
food eaten. People aren't any more satisfied after a meal if they ate more(!) The
satisfaction comes from the satiation of the food, not from the amount consumed.
headings: 3. Save up calories, but don’t starve yourself

pages.text:
5

After the cheat meal, definitely don't force yourself afterwards to eat more fat or
carbohydrate just to hit your daily macro target. Not gaining fat is generally far more
important than your carb or fat targets. The top priority in practice is always to minimize
the overeating.

6

4. Get in the right mindset
"The meal isn't over when I'm full, the meal is over when I hate myself."
Those are comedian Louis C. K.'s famous words to exemplify the effect of your mindset
on how much you eat. Many trainees either eat super 'clean' and restrained or they
completely let go of the brakes and try to stuff as much food into themselves as they
can. If you want to stay very lean year-round and enjoy your diet, you'll have to find a
middle ground here.

Think about what your goal of the cheat meal is. Assuming it's to enjoy food, realize
there's a difference between being satiated and "I'm so stuffed I can't walk anymore
without stomach pain." Is force-feeding that last plate of dessert really going to make
you happier?

7

5. Do this before you order anything
Ask yourself: how many calories per 100 gram of food are roughly in this food? Be
objective and add at least a 20% error margin to account for hidden calories from sugar,
butters and oils. Most chefs use very large amounts of fats to prepare their meals and
this quickly racks up extra calories.

Estimating the calorie density rather than the total calorie intake is often much more
accurate. Trying to estimate the total number of calories in a meal is extremely difficult if
you haven't weighted the foods and don't know the exact ingredients, but to estimate
the energy density, you just need to know the primary ingredients. The calorie density is
strongly correlated with the meal's satiety index and thereby how many calories you'll
likely end up consuming if you eat this until you're full.

Having an idea of how many calories are in the meal is in turn crucial to determine if the
meal is worth it.

As a reference, research finds that a typical dinner meal for the average person has
around 1200 kcal, so strength trainees that don't pay strict attention to their food
choices will often need to budget this as a minimum.
headings: 5. Do this before you order anything

pages.text:
8

6. Protein and fiber first, treats second
Protein and fiber are highly thermogenic. This means your body expends a lot of energy
during their digestion. You can make your body burn up to 20% more calories by eating
a lot of fiber and protein in a meal.

Protein is also anabolic. If you don't consume enough protein with a highly caloric meal,
you can be assured that most of it will be stored as fat. With protein in the meal you will
stimulate muscle growth.

Moreover, protein and fiber are very satiating and good for your health.

So eat lean protein sources, vegetables and fibrous fruits first. Once you've got your
protein and fiber in, then you can decide if you want to enjoy any higher calorie treats.
It's crucial to consume your fillers and protein first, before you've started eating your
treats, for several reasons.
1. It ensures you actually consume them.
2. You enjoy saltier, whole foods much more when you're still hungry and your taste
receptors aren't spoiled by higher calorie treats and sweets yet.
3. You can make much more rational decisions about what else you may want to
eat when you're no longer hungry.
headings: 6. Protein and fiber first, treats second

pages.text:
9

7. Be selective
Your brain regulates your appetite by estimating food availability. Your brain also has a
sensory-specific appetite suppression after food intake. As a result of this, new tastes
partially 'reset' your appetite and the larger the variety of foods you eat within a meal,
the greater your appetite and the more likely you are to overeat. Even the illusion of a
high variety of food has been found to increase energy intake by 43%.

So before you start eating in any place, assess which foods are available. Carefully
determine which of them are really worth it.
headings: high variety of food has been found to increase energy intake by 43%.

pages.text:
10

8. Focus on your food
The difference between pigging out and enjoying a cheat meal is mindfulness. During
social occasions people can eat over 75% more than when alone. A primary reason is
that they're distracted. When you are distracted, your brain literally doesn't register food
intake as effectively. Even worse, you don't even enjoy your food. What's the point of a
cheat meal if you're not enjoying the food?

So if you find yourself eating and talking at the same time, you're doing it wrong. You
should enjoy the company and the food, just not at the same time. Didn't your mother
teach you not to talk with a full mouth?

11

9. Train on cheat meal days
Most gyms are closed during public holidays, but that doesn't mean you can't stimulate
your muscles. Even light exercise at just 15% of your max has been found to
significantly increase protein building in muscle tissue after a meal. At just 30% of your
1 repetition maximum, going to failure can make the exercise as effective as regular
weight training for muscle growth.

Exercise reduces the damage from overeating in several ways.
• Increased energy expenditure reduces the energy surplus.
• Improved nutrient partitioning means more of the energy surplus will be directed
towards muscle growth and less towards fat storage.
• Exercise improves your brain's satiety regulation in the post-workout period.

If you overeat outside your anabolic windows, basically all energy intake over
maintenance will be stored. If you trained beforehand, not only is your maintenance
energy intake higher and you're less likely to overeat due to greater satiety sensitivity,
you'll also store a significantly greater part of your energy surplus as lean body mass.

So consider doing some exercise beforehand if you're going to set an all-you-can-eat
record. Lack of equipment is a lame excuse. Even with nothing but bodyweight you can
generally get a good workout in. You can always do push-ups, pull-ups, sprints, one-
legged squats, hip thrusts, calf raises, planks, straight-legged bridges, handstand push-
ups, etc.

12

10. Understand alcohol
The scientific definition of binge drinking is drinking more than a single consumption of
alcohol as a woman or 2 consumptions as a man. Per day. If you're going binge
drinking, check out Menno's article on The science of binge drinking: 7 Tips to get
wasted without wasting your gains.

13

11. Serve yourself small portion sizes
Food availability is not the only cue your brain uses to regulate your appetite. It also
estimates how much you've eaten based on contextual cues. These include the size of
your plate and cutlery and the amount of food per serving. Because our brain uses the
amount of food we're served as a reference of how much to eat, we suffer from
something called the 'clean plate effect': we tend to finish our plate even when we're no
longer really hungry.

The solution: Eat with smaller cutlery and eat smaller servings on small plates. You'll
intuitively eat less but become just as satiated and satisfied. Especially if you're going to
a self-serve setting like a buffet or Christmas dinner with your family, serve yourself
small portion sizes: the minimal amount if it's something new you want to try and just
enough to satiate you if you already know you like it. This helps you try foods without
being forced to consume a lot of them and it's polite to boot. Since you can always
serve more food later, small serving sizes are typically a no-risk, high-reward strategy.

14

12. Drink plenty of zero-calorie fluids during your meal
Water, (decaf) tea and coffee and calorie free sodas are a great way to increase the
volume and satiety of your meals without adding calories. They can also slightly
increase the thermogenic effect of the meal. So don't skimp on the drinks.

If drinks are expensive, and they usually are outrageously so, ask for a big bottle/carafe
of tap water, assuming that's safe to drink in the country you're in. If you ask directly,
you can often get it for free, especially if you or someone else does order a paid drink. If
you think it will be frowned upon, try first asking for something that you're sure they
don't have, like a Schweppes diet ginger ale or a diet Lipton Iced Tea. When they can't
offer you that, there's extra social pressure not to refuse you some simple tap water.
headings: 12. Drink plenty of zero-calorie fluids during your meal

pages.text:
15

13. Doggy-bag it
While perhaps not as elegant as 'eating like a Frenchman', doggy-bagging your food is
an effective strategy to reduce overeating on large serving sizes and prevent the Clean
Plate Effect. Packaging and storing your food for later consumption reduces our
tendency to overeat large portion sizes. Knowing that we can doggy-bag our food, or at
home store it in the fridge, lets us eat intuitively until we're full. We can focus on
enjoying our food and stop eating when we no longer need more food, rather than
mindlessly finishing whatever we're served. So whenever you're in a restaurant and
you're full, stop eating and ask for a doggy-bag (or have a waiter take away the food).
At home, remember you have a loyal fridge standing by to help you with your diet.

If you have food left that you don't want to eat anymore, not now or at home, it can help
to move it away from you across the table, out of arm's reach. This serves as a
psychological rejection of the food. You can also messy up the food by, for example,
putting a napkin in it. Making the food look unappealing triggers what psychologists call
the 'messy satiation' effect due to a mild disgust emotion.

16

Conclusion
Be mindful of how and what you eat, employ subtle self-trickery and apply the tips from
this guide to enjoy your social eating events without getting fat: 0% guilt, 100%
satisfaction.

Enjoy the food!

all_headings: 2. Don’t be a slave to the menu
3. Save up calories, but don’t starve yourself
5. Do this before you order anything
6. Protein and fiber first, treats second
high variety of food has been found to increase energy intake by 43%.
12. Drink plenty of zero-calorie fluids during your meal
